results.english:
…to visit the famous temple of Gorakshanatha.

Dozens of Natha-yogins in saffron robes, with the great kundala-earrings, filled the courtyard of the temple complex. Looking at them, he thought that the very Yoga which had raised the Nathas to spiritual heights ought to have remained their priority — not political games, not the management of a vast network of ashrams, not the printing and distribution of sacred texts for profit. And certainly not cunning deals in land and real estate. All these practical aspects were understandable enough, but the essence of the teaching had nearly vanished, had been forgotten, pushed into the background. Like many other religious organizations in modern India, the Nathas had to survive, and what mattered most was fundraising, not Yoga. Now it had become a relic, a by-product of past greatness.

And yet one could still trace the roots of Hatha-Yoga, which reached down into the Natha Sampradaya. Although Kriya Yoga is not directly tied to the Natha tradition, strict discipline and devotion to the goal of realization are branches of the same great tree of Knowledge. The practice of the Nathas is rather complex and demands a discipline of which renunciation of society is a part. Few modern seekers can follow such vows. As a rule, interaction with the world is curtailed; ascetics are expected to keep themselves apart and to follow the rules prescribed by the tradition.

The Natha maths-monasteries still preserve old traditions, but this does not always mean that yogic practices are maintained within them. Only a few yogins in the Natha sampradaya still possess the true knowledge. And although the Nathas are distinct from other sadhus, finding a practicing yogin among them is not easy. In his view, the sole criterion of realization was an unbroken yogic practice.

For even respected spiritual communities with well-established hierarchies cannot carry the essence of the teaching into the future, and the authority and reputation of a saint are not based on membership in any organization. The immense effort required for realization comes from personal attainment. Practice unveils aspects of knowledge that cannot be taught in words; they can only be earned in person, in the course of one's own tapas.

…They walked out of the temple, and the Nathas seated in the courtyard fell silent, staring openly at the strings of rudraksha around his neck and at his long hair. He did not look back; the energies of the place were somber, half-questioning, half-rejecting.

Gorakhpur is located not far from the Nepalese border, and they took a rickshaw to the checkpoint. The border guards let them through without any delay, simply waving them on. He had no documents, not even a driver's license, but at that time travel into Nepal was not hedged in with formalities. In the nearest town they boarded a bus to Kathmandu and dozed for the next ten hours, though they were periodically jolted on the hard seats. After a while the rhythm of the road shifted, softened by the breakwaters of the mountains and the narrowness of the green valleys. The highway grew smoother, and he fell asleep…

The next day they entered the main building of the temple complex at Pashupatinath, where he introduced himself to the chief pandit, handing him a copy of his commentary on the Gita. He explained that he needed to see the old Bhatt-ji (such was the title of the religious head of Pashupatinath). Taking the book carefully, the priest asked him to wait, and returned after almost an hour. He was invited into the living quarters behind the temple.

…Bhatt-ji was already 99 years old, and very weak. Owing to his health the chief pandit no longer received visitors, but the Gita brought by the yogin from Govardhan moved the old priest to meet the author.

All rituals at Pashupatinath are performed by two groups of priests: the Bhattas and the Rajbhandari. The Bhattas — highly educated Vedic Dravidian Brahmins from Karnataka — are carefully selected and dispatched to Nepal to perform the daily rituals. [footnote] The temple of Pashupatinath is the oldest and one of the most sacred Hindu temples. The complex stands on both banks of the Bagmati river on the eastern edge of Kathmandu and is dedicated to Lord Shiva. In 1979 it was inscribed as a World Heritage site. The place attracts hundreds of elderly Hindus who come here to spend the last weeks of their lives and to meet death, in order to be cremated on the bank of the river. It is believed that those who die at Pashupatinath are reborn as human beings regardless of the misdeeds that may have worsened their karma. The exact day of death is foretold by the temple astrologers. [/footnote] Initiated into Pashupata Yoga and the Shiva Agamas, they must satisfy many requirements before they may touch the idol. The Bhandari caste is not allowed to perform puja; they are mainly assistants and caretakers.

…The old man lay on the bed; beside him sat two rather ancient servants. In a cracked but still authoritative voice the priest asked in Hindi: "What do you want?" And he told everything: of the finding of the Shivalingam and Its installation at the cremation site of the Raja, of the illness that afflicted all who touched It, even of the death of the dog. But, most of all, of the voice that had sent him to Pashupatinath.

The words came clearly and distinctly, yet he had the strange impression that the story was being told by someone else. He finished; the old priest was silent for almost ten minutes. No one moved. A solitary fly was studying an unlit lamp, confidently sinking its proboscis into the viscous surface of the dark oil. Then Bhatt-ji began to dictate, and for a start ordered him to wear red clothes during the ritual. Bewildered by this unexpected recommendation, he asked: "Why red?" Hindus associate this color with tantric ceremonies, but Bhatt-ji gave an altogether different explanation. During a Hindu wedding the husband is regarded as Parameshvara Himself, a form of Lord Shiva. [footnote] Parameshvara — the supreme God or supreme Being. In Hinduism it most often refers to Lord Shiva. [/footnote] Grateful for the opportunity to be married to Divinity Itself in the image of her husband, the bride puts on a red sari.

Bhatt-ji tried to raise himself on the bed, looking intently at him: "To find a natural Shivalingam, Parameshvara, is a great fortune and an honor for a mortal. When you worship Him on a smashana, wear red as a sign of fortune, of gratitude, and of devotion." The long, detailed explanation of every aspect of the ritual took almost an hour, and he wrote it down diligently, trying not to miss anything. When the dictation ended, he bowed and walked out of the dark room, carrying the precious notebook. The old priest refused to accept money, but he nevertheless dropped 500 rupees into the donation box at the entrance.

The next day they flew to Benares, and instead of a passport he showed the border guard the spread of the Bhagavad Gita with his own photograph: "In this photograph it is I, Shailendra Sharma…". The small plane smelled of cardamom and old newspapers.

In Benares, after visiting the Vishwanath temple, he stopped at the house of Lahiri Mahasaya to see Banamali Lahiri, the great-great-grandson of the Master. As usual, the conversation was warm and engaging. Even so, he decided not to mention either the Shivalingam or the purpose of his trip to Nepal. Religious and esoteric subjects interested the professor little; he was inclined toward philosophy rather than metaphysics…

…Worship of Shiva began on the first day of the Dussehra festival. In accordance with the instructions he had received, the puja was performed twice a day, and for the first two weeks he was a little nervous, trying not to confuse the sequence. To hold simultaneously a pitcher of milk and the horn through which the offering was made was rather awkward, but Devahuti and Mahatma-ji helped him until the ritual became second nature.

…That day, around noon, they were sitting on the terrace when a child's voice came from the temple: "Har, Har, Mahadev!" Then followed the dry rattle of a drum. Going to investigate, they saw a boy dressed as Shiva standing in front of the Shivalingam. The child apparently belonged to a troupe of wandering performers, who usually beg for alms at places of pilgrimage in costumes of Indian gods.

While his companions rested in the dharamshala in front of the gates of the smashana, the boy had made friends with the unsmiling guard and asked permission to enter the temple. His thin body was painted blue; around his slender neck hung a necklace of rudraksha. Bathed in sunlight, with a lit stick of incense somehow fixed in his hair, he held a slim iron trident in his hand, and the incense smoke filled the air with a thick fragrance. Not trying to pose or to act, the child-performer simply soaked up that day — the birdsong, the light, everything around him. They gave him a few coins, and accepting them with dignity and gratitude, the boy bowed reservedly to the Shivalingam. And left. Sweet and unremarkable as this small incident was, something had shifted, as though the temple had been consecrated once again.

…In every temple dedicated to Shiva there is an oval stone that is worshipped as Mahadeva Himself. Of varying size, natural or hand-carved, the Shivalingam is usually installed in the center of a disc-shaped platform — the yoni. This installation symbolizes the merging of the micro- and macrocosm, the union of feminine and masculine, the eternal process of creation and rebirth. For the Shaivites, the lingam-yoni is a metaphor of spiritual truths and faith.

From the nineteenth century onward, various sources (mostly in Western literature) described the Shivalingam as a reproductive organ, the phallus of Shiva. This crude interpretation contradicts the higher meaning of Shaivism, in which the lingam-yoni stands for the dual principles of creation — different yet united elements and principles. The Skanda Purana states that all beings possess the attributes of Shiva and Shakti; all creatures display the duality of Nature.

For some, the Shivalingam may mean a part of Shiva's body; for the more discerning seeker, it is a symbol of the presence of Shiva-Time. Everything depends on the level of development. In South India the Tamil Shaivites call the Shivalingam kuri, "sign" or "marker" — and perceive Him exclusively as a spiritual symbol, without any sexual implication. For devotees of the Destroyer, the Shivalingam symbolizes the axis of creation, the passage from one reality to another.

Each night, touching the smooth oval surface, he felt that the stone radiated something that could only be described as the energy of eternity. Without trying to decode all these intricate metaphors and words, he simply felt the presence and the breath of Shiva. And he became one with Him. In his sadhana he felt a power ripening within him; the will, bound together by yoga and worship, became a silent witness to the mystery. The unconscious expanded until no mind remained at all, and only the soundless voice of Shiva-Time spoke with him through the lips of Prana.

The Heart of the Earth

The very first story of true love was the story of Shiva and Sati. [footnote] Sati — one of the aspects of Shakti. The first wife of Shiva, whom she married against the will of her father Daksha. [/footnote] When She performed self-immolation, grieving Shiva began to wander the world, carrying Her corpse. Mahadeva vowed not to stop until His beloved's body had disintegrated entirely — such was the depth of His love, the magnitude of the loss… The gods asked Vishnu to halt this terrifying march of mourning, and the Preserver of the Universe severed Shakti's body with the sharp disc of the Sudarshana-chakra into 51 parts. One after another, they fell to the earth, and so the sacred places were born — the Shakti Pithas. [footnote] Shakti Pitha — when Sati ended her life, grief overwhelmed Shiva; He carried His wife's body, refusing to part with Her. This might have led to the destruction of the world, and so Lord Vishnu severed Sati's body into 51 parts, which fell upon the earth in various places. These sacred places became known as the Shakti Pithas. [/footnote] The feminine force of the Goddess passed into the Earth, becoming a part of it, and these ancient sites of eternal creative power are still revered by millions of pilgrims, symbolizing the force of love, pride, loss, and eternal union.

Across the Indian subcontinent 51 Shakti Pithas are counted, by other reckonings 108. The four most important and sacred are situated in Odisha, Assam, and Bengal, and Braj is not on this list. Nevertheless, in the ancient…

results.english:
…tantric text, the Yogini Tantra, there is a curious mention. Apart from religious and philosophical themes, this voluminous work — devoted to the worship of Kali and Kamakhya — contains some startling historical material. In it Govardhan is named as Kamakhya, the important tantric goddess of creative force, desire, and fertility.

Both physical and metaphysical signs of the Goddess's presence on the smashana appeared too often to be ignored. Yet to weave all the events into a single fabric was almost impossible. The mixture of myths, anthropological evidence, religious convictions, intuition, and mysticism had been fermenting for years; one needed to digest and assimilate all this data so that it could become wisdom. That is precisely what he was doing, but, whatever his conclusions, he kept them to himself.

Such revelations could not lead either lay people or academic researchers to constructive conclusions. People do not like to see widely accepted facts being challenged. Once approved and documented, history must become an undisputable postulate, signed and catalogued like a fossil. It is generally considered that disputed facts ought to remain veiled or to take the modest guise of alternative hypotheses.

The very name of the locality was a key to the riddle of the Shakti Pithas. A portion of the Skanda Purana, the text "Bhagavad Mahatmya," gave hints as to the origin of the name "Moksh Bhumi" in the legend of the last journey of the queens — the widows of Krishna. When, having completed His mission, Krishna left the body, His grandson Vajranabh inherited the kingdom of Mathura. Inconsolable, their hearts broken by grief, 16,107 widowed queens mourned Krishna. But Queen Yamuna did not look despondent, and the other widows wondered: "Why are you not grieving — do you not feel the pain of separation?"

Yamuna could not hide her surprise: "Do you not see Him? He is here!" — and she advised them to seek guidance from Shandilya Rishi, who could help the queens to be reunited with Krishna. No one in Braj ever says that Krishna died (what a base thought!). It is believed that He simply interrupted His divine Lila — and so there was nothing unusual in the queens' hope of seeing Him again.

The Rishi told them to hold festivities at the foot of the hill Govardhan, on the shore of lake Kusum Sarovar — there, where Krishna's immortal friend Uddhava lived. As the Rishi explained to the widows, Uddhava could endow them with the hidden knowledge of the Maha Bhagavad Gita, which would help them to find peace. Following the advice, the queens pitched a luxurious camp between lakes Manasi Ganga and Kusum Sarovar, and Uddhava — dark-skinned, radiant, dressed in golden robes, strikingly resembling Krishna — read them the Maha Bhagavad Gita for a whole month. And they all began to see Krishna everywhere. As it is said, they attained "Krishna consciousness," which in fact means that they left their earthly bodies. There the chapter ended.

All 16,107 queens were cremated on the plot of land between the royal smashana and the lake Kusum Sarovar. In the local dialect this area is known as Shaktitara, which means "the place where wives obtained liberation." Most likely, the origin of the name "Moksh Bhumi" is connected with this story.

Aware of the powerful energies of the place, an unknown sadhu marked the boundaries of the future dynastic cremation complex and bestowed on thirteen generations of the rulers of Bharatpur a priceless gift — release from rebirth. Powerful ancient feminine energies guarded and nourished this land, and in combination with the energy of the holy hill produced an indescribable effect — a sense of pure, inexplicable, but unmistakable grace. And of peace.

…The numerous Shakti Pithas across India are dedicated to different parts of Sati's body, and each locality has its own meaning. In the state of Jharkhand, the temple of Jayadurga in Baidyanath is held to be the "official" place where the heart of Sati is believed to have fallen. The site is widely known as the land of the sacred heart (Baidyanath Dham, or Hridaya Pitha). Yet the pulse of the metaphysical, mystical Heart of the Earth is felt everywhere.

As is known, the planet pulses with a particular kind of resonant waves called Schumann resonance — this is the "heartbeat of the Earth." These waves are regulated by electricity. Lightning striking the ground creates electromagnetic waves in the atmosphere. Held between the earth and the upper layers of the atmosphere, these waves do not race across the surface of the planet but produce a kind of standing waves, pulsating in their troughs and crests, and this resonant "heartbeat" pumps prana into the body of the Earth.

The heartbeat of the planet vibrates everywhere; in Braj it is felt especially strongly. Coming from all directions, it passes through trees, animals, birds, people, and inanimate objects. Invisible, the great Heart continues to work in harmony with the rhythm of Creation, and the tiny motors within all living beings beat in unison with the heart of the Mother.

…Sacred places are territories where religions and beliefs interact with nature. Only human cultures can confer such statuses upon natural objects, for only faith rooted in legends that were once someone's reality has enduring meaning. All these spatial markers — from the significant sites of the cradle of Christian and Jewish faith and the holy places of Islam, down to the hills and rivers honored by aboriginal and primal tribes — reflect human sensitivity to events of the past, filtered by time and charged with the power of the earth.

Of course, the value of any shrine and the multitude of its meanings exists exclusively in the eyes of the believers. Human hearts always thirst for miracles; people are born to believe — and they are given temples of valleys and mountains beneath the domes of the open skies, where any heart may connect with the primal Source, the heart of the Mother. The whole Earth is an enormous temple, consecrated by the Void in which She floats. Perhaps the heart of the Earth shifts its position within the body of the planet too, depending on the course of events, its trajectory and destination?

Wherever it may be, the exact location of the planet's heart is unknown, and people are not destined to learn of it. Here, in Braj, the Govardhan Hill has been worshipped for many centuries, and there was no need for new hypotheses and legends. The Shakti Pitha of Braj remained invisible, continuing to be charged with the energy of millions of pilgrims. The grace of the chief Shakti, the Earth, was meant for everyone, and She continued to carry all Her children.

Devi of Braj

The dilapidated wooden pavilion above the cremation site of Raja Jaswant Singh was erected in 1893. Half-ruined, covered in flaking red paint, the structure stood in the midst of the deserted, scorched earth, guarded by a single palm tree. When he saw the pagoda-like structure for the first time, a sudden revelation flashed through him: "The Shivalingam will stand right here." There was no clarity about the origin of this idea, but in conversation with Devahuti he shared the thought that the pavilion would be turned into a temple. She listened in silence, asking nothing. She had her hands full with endless practical matters of setting up the new household.

The pujari, who had moved with them onto the smashana, had previously worked in metal but also understood building. They often sat on the upper level of the palace, discussing the reconstruction of the structure, and in this way considered many variants. He had initially planned to buy and install a Shivalingam (more precisely a Banalingam) from the sacred river Narmada. When the symbol of Shiva was found in their own "backyard," they took it as a sign that the time had come to refit the building.

The repairs were discussed and refined so many times that the work was done quickly and without trouble. But no one could then have foreseen the significance of the events to which the consecration of a Shiva temple at the place of the Raja's samadhi would give rise.

Setting aside his reflections on the nature of this thoroughly tantric ritual, he began performing puja to Shiva, fully immersed in the new daily routine. Perhaps his unawareness was a blessing. To outsiders all these events had no particular meaning, but he was sure: Shiva had come to the Smashana because of Shakti. The discovery of the Shivalingam pointed to His intent to remain here, and Her presence had always been felt; She was the true mistress of this land. From time immemorial various ancient feminine spirits (besides the souls of Krishna's wives cremated here) had inhabited Braj. Saturated with their mighty energies, the locality was rich in mysteries.

When Shakti came to Braj, Her presence filled the surroundings with intense vibrations, which made newcomers giddy and gave them a state akin to intoxication. The sensation of intoxication without the aid of any liquor confused and disoriented many, but those who often visited the smashana were well acquainted with the strange energies and looked forward to meeting them.

That this place was a Shakti Pitha he understood when proofs began to surface — literally. The signs of Shakti's presence were discovered on December 1, 1994, during routine earthworks, when the workmen began to come upon strange stone fragments of a peculiar configuration — exact copies of female reproductive organs, breasts and the birthing organ, yoni. On the stone breasts nipples appeared with reddish areolae, as though grown into the stone, and all these fragments were patently not made by human hand. They continued to be unearthed, and within a few weeks more than thirty pieces of different sizes and shapes had been collected. The stones were washed, cleaned of clay, and gathered in the upper room. Trying to understand the meaning of these finds, he consulted with archaeologists and reviewed a great deal of specialized literature, but could not decipher the hidden meanings. The answers were oblique, evasive; possible theories and explanations varied.

The workers themselves, however, showed no surprise: it is well known that in Braj the sacred stones of the Govardhan hill behave in the most unpredictable manner. Some stones are even considered to be holy hermits and sages performing thousand-year tapas in this disguised form, moving along and around the Hill. The legend-like phenomenon was well known here — stones did indeed move, some could be found meters (or kilometers) from the places where they had been marked. According to age-old tradition, people draw eyes upon them, apply orange sindur paste, and worship them… But the stone fragments found on the smashana had a quite different, undeniably "feminine" nature.

…Two months later (on January 27, 1995), the guard brought a strange, half-buried object found at the gate. An oblong oval stone about 30 centimeters long, it was surprisingly light. Yielding to an impulse, he dropped it on the stone floor, and the upper part broke off. A small bronze face peered out from the broken surface.

Convinced that this was not stone but fossilized wax mixed with sindur, Devahuti placed the rest of the object in a large pot and set it on a low flame. Soon the wax melted, revealing a small bronze statuette of a four-armed goddess seated upon a great cat, distantly resembling a tiger. The Devi held a sword, a cup, and a strange round object resembling a doughnut. The statuette was placed on a shelf in the upper room, and from that day the Goddess silently, though attentively, watched his nightly sadhana.

The statuette and the strange stone fragments were tokens of Her arrival, but the Goddess Herself did not intend to prove the fact of Her presence to anyone. She simply filled the whole locality with the intoxicating sweetness of a sharp sense of being.

So far as he could tell, all these deceptively chance events caused great upheavals in the astral spheres.

In October 1995 Hanuman spoke with him personally for the first time, and this happened because of Shakti's arrival. But he understood that only later. Testing and guiding him, the Deity explained the reasons for some of the astonishing events.

results.english:
And then, two months later, Babaji himself appeared at the smashana. Of course the Mahavatar was well aware of past, present, and future events and, most likely, influenced many of them. That the companion of Babaji brought here the symbol of the Goddess, the Shri Yantra, was no accident at all.

…On January 4, 1996, while the pond was being drained, yet another stone fragment resembling a female reproductive organ was found. Imagination had no part in the discovery; there were too many fragments simply to wave aside, to dismiss as random natural formations. The Earth was no longer shyly presenting "evidence" of the Divine — She was proclaiming it with all the passion of feminine energy.

Soon the forked trunks of trees in the garden of the smashana began to "bleed"; from them oozed a dark, viscous, resin-like substance. The unusual phenomenon occurred in particular months, as if coinciding with the lunar cycles. Devahuti noticed it first and pointed it out to him. First stone breasts, then yonis, and now menstruating trees? That would be enough to set the locals making ambiguous jokes at his expense.

…Six months passed, and in June Mahadeva Himself stood in the darshan hall. The unimaginable event was piercingly real; he could still feel the burning touch of His sweat on his own skin. Sadhana had taught him that reality is divided into many channels, and he had never tried to confine himself to the limits of the mind, since precisely the breaking of limits meant going beyond the body.

The events that followed gave some answers, though the divine play unfolded slowly. Less than two years (a fraction of a second by the time of the gods) after that, the Shivalingam was found, which expanded his duties beyond mere teaching and personal practice. The daily worship of Shiva became the occasion for beginning communication with the subtle spheres; a new stage of his training began. It was then that it became clear: Adi Shakti Herself had called Her beloved here.

In 2016 a well-known sculptor made a large copy of the statuette found at the gate. The Devi still had no name, but it did not particularly matter: She personified the Sacred Feminine and required no credentials or documents. By that time ten different yantras had already arrived at the smashana; the Mahavidyas, various personifications and aspects of the Goddess, had been reunited with Her.

The statue was installed in the southern hall of the house; seated upon an enormous cat, the Goddess gazed silently with diamond-inset eyes upon the room, which was carpeted in red. A new, dense, honey-like energy filled the surroundings.

On October 28, 2020, during another round of pond-clearing work (thanks to the COVID quarantine), a new stone fragment was discovered. Rather dark, the size of a small grapefruit, with a dimple. Inserting his thumb into the dimple — about a centimeter and a half deep — he felt the stone pulsing. This was the navel of Shakti. The stone was carefully washed in milk and water and placed beside the other "body parts" and yantras. The symbols of the creative feminine power, translated by Nature into objects intelligible to human beings, were now together: "breast," "yoni," "navel."

Such stories were meant for those who had traveled with him for a long while and could look behind the thin veil of reality. Only close friends and some of the students could grasp the intricate interweaving of many legends, catch the meaning of various events, and try to analyze them.

The practical aspects of life eclipsed the tales of miracles; everything improbable was set aside as the whim of imagination; the ordinary was prized as the bulwark of safety and the guarantor of daily stability. People came to him to study yoga, to receive teaching, technique, and inspiration, and he gave all this, willingly fulfilling his duties and telling newcomers the stories of the smashana only in moderate doses. Just enough to kindle their curiosity, to draw them out of the ordinary.

Gradually the very sequence of events reshuffled the deck of plausible conclusions. Traditional Hindu iconography depicts the Goddess Kali, the fierce form of Shakti, standing on a motionless body. Under Her feet there is usually a shava (any dead body) or even the sleeping Shiva Himself. The Dark Goddess either takes the mortal's soul or holds Shiva back from awakening. In this way She prevents His dance of destruction, the Tandava, so that Creation may continue.

In Hindu temples Shakti is represented as the yoni, which is the base, the foundation, supporting the Shivalingam.

Although the convulsions of Indian history have provided a great many dead bodies, no known instance had ever been recorded of the installation of a Shivalingam-and-Yoni over a Shava-corpse. The union of the great forces always needs a "launching pad," and the Smashana provided such a place. Now Shiva and Shakti stood on the very spot where the last funeral rite of the ruler of Bharatpur had been performed — all three components of the tantric mystery were actively engaged. The spirit of the Raja was in excellent company — standing above his tomb, the eternal Goddess held Shiva-Time firmly and tenderly in Her embrace.

The Three Nathas

Guru Purnima is celebrated annually throughout India; on this day of honoring teachers, thousands of people express their gratitude to all mentors, including spiritual ones. The festival is also dedicated to the birthday of the sage Vyasa, the author of the most important texts of Hinduism: the Puranas, the Mahabharata, and the Vedas. The literal translation of the Sanskrit word "Guru" means "the one who delivers from ignorance." Gu is darkness, ru is light.

Guru Purnima usually falls on the full moon of the month of Ashadha, and on July 8, 1998, hundreds of thousands of people flooded Govardhan. Amid the festive activities, beneath the canopy of the dharamshala that stood opposite the gates of the smashana, three pilgrims had settled in. One of them at once lay down and did not rise again; his companion settled beside him, continuously smoking chillum and watching the passing crowds. The third sadhu kept visiting a refreshment stall at the corner and conversing there with the vendor. The next day the third one appeared at the gates of the smashana and asked permission to see the temple of Shiva. He told the gatekeeper that he had heard from the locals of the installation of the Shivalingam, and the gatekeeper led him to the temple, where the visitor suddenly fell unconscious. Collapsing on the floor, he lay senseless for a few moments, but the man accompanying him was not especially worried, since the force of the dark oval stone, recently installed in the sanctum, often had a similar effect on people. Coming to, the pilgrim asked to meet the "Elder Natha" (so he called the owner of the smashana).

Approaching the visitor, he caught a flicker of instant recognition in the stranger's eyes. Looking at the dress and accessories of the ascetic, one could guess: he was a Natha-yogin. The guest wore only a sun-bleached turban and a lungi wrapped around his muscular, lean thighs. Large round kundala earrings, the distinguishing symbols of the Nathas, were inserted not in the earlobes but through the cartilage, in the manner of the followers of Gorakshanatha. The large kundalas, made of a light-grayish material resembling bone, did not seem heavy at all.

The Natha bowed in pranama, and that was strange, since these yogins bow to no one; their traditional greeting is limited to the word "Adesh." [footnote] Adesh — a common greeting among the Nathas. The "Siddha Siddhanta Paddhati" states that "Adesh" means the unity of Atma (soul), Jivatma (soul-spirit), and Paramatma (Highest, universal Spirit). [/footnote] Introducing himself as Ganganatha, the visitor briefly mentioned his companions: the silent chillum-smoker was named Darshaninatha, and the sleeping man was their teacher, Chandnatha. The guest looked entirely self-assured and somehow recalled a friendly jinn. Addressing him as "Badda Natha" — "Elder Natha" — the guest at once began to praise him for carrying on the tradition of the Nathas, and for a moment he thought the visitor was about to ask for money. As if reading his thoughts, the Natha mentioned with cheerful ease that he and his companions had come on business, looking for a suitable plot of land; they were going to build an ashram there, and the price was of no importance. With these words Ganganatha demonstrated, hidden beneath his loincloth, a money belt stuffed with new banknotes.

The Natha spoke of most interesting things, and he could not catch all the hints, allegories, and metaphors, but in time everything the guest told him turned out to be precisely what it was — incredible truth.

"You installed the Shivalingam on the smashana — and therefore you are the Natha of all Nathas. For any mortal this is a great honor and a great trial. In the past many Natha-yogins performed tapas at cremation grounds, trying to call forth the Avadhuta. Some attained the highest states, becoming like Rishis, but the Guru never honored them with His manifestation." Listening to Ganganatha, he was sure that the guest was speaking exclusively of Shiva, for whom else could one call "Guru"? The years and events that followed disclosed a more complex and many-layered cosmogony, in which different deities and forces took part.

Both Shiva and Dattatreya are called "Avadhuta" — most often this is the title of Lord Shiva, and in India such coincidences trouble no one. So called is the master who shows the way of liberation, the mystic who has gone beyond duality, or the saint who teaches seekers to realize the true nature of the highest reality and of themselves.

Saying that the installation of the Shivalingam at the cremation site of the Raja had awakened the Avadhuta, and that such an event occurs only once in a cycle of creation, Ganganatha was speaking of Dattatreya — the mystical three-headed Guru revered by the Nathas. One of Dattatreya's three heads embodies Shiva-Aghora, the fierce and beautiful aspect of the Destroyer, in which He meditates upon smashanas. While the other heads sleep, one is awake, influencing the events and changes of the Universe — and now Shiva-Aghora had awakened, which meant the beginning of a new era.

Suddenly the guest spoke of the appearance and meaning of the signs of Shakti recently found by the workmen, and to hear this was astonishing, for the Natha could not have known of it; only a handful of people were aware of the find of the stone fragments.

"Mahadeva came here because here, on the smashana, Shakti had already manifested…" That was a striking declaration, but he himself had been working out the same version.

Their exchange proved intriguingly many-sided: obvious conclusions and statements alternated with veiled hints, leaving room for further reflection. To round off the conversation Ganganatha asked permission to rest somewhere on the grounds of the smashana, away from the noise of the crowded streets, and the pujari led him to a secluded veranda of the palace, where the guest remained for a couple of hours.

The next morning all three Nathas simply dissolved into the hot July haze. The stone floor where Ganganatha had lain had turned white, as if someone had outlined his silhouette in chalk; the faintly visible discolored lines vanished with the coming of the monsoon.

…Ganganatha had mentioned the town of Bhatinda in Punjab: "Ask anyone — everyone there knows me." This visit and conversation were so intriguing that he asked a relative, a major serving in Bhatinda, to make inquiries. The local people led the major to a local landmark — an ancient tomb, a site of yogic samadhi. Did it belong to that very Ganganatha who had recently come to the smashana? Absurd as the guess might sound, it somehow seemed plausible.

An excursion into the history and hierarchy of the Nathas brought further surprises. Darshaninatha possessed the gift of influence over the mass media: television channels, journalists, and internet bloggers came under his sway. Their Guru, Chandnatha, controlled politicians and was a direct disciple of the ancient immortal Gorakshanatha. The surviving archival records mentioned that Chandnatha was several thousand years old, and that he possessed the secrets of attaining the immortal body, which had been taught to him by Smashana Kali herself, the fierce dark Goddess. These three wandering Nathas belonged to the branch of…

results.english:
…the Kaya Natha-yogins, possessors of the secrets of eternal life.

…Recalling Ganganatha's bow, he always felt a certain quiver — for it is not every day that an immortal being bows to you. This incident gave him a strange certainty, as though the navigation chart of events had become less blurred, more distinct. Not that he needed encouragement or confirmation of the rightness of his actions — and yet…

From time to time he had a fleeting sense, as if the Nathas were somewhere very near — watching, waiting, perhaps passing the gates of the Smashana mixed with hundreds of other pilgrims. Unrecognized and nameless, forever merged with the vastness of the subcontinent, the eternal witnesses of the Dharma, the immortals continued to wander among the living.

The Yantras

Over the course of several years, in different but equally strange circumstances, eleven yantras manifested on the royal smashana. Their purpose was well known, but the reasons for their appearance remained a riddle for a long while. Visitors put forward various hypotheses, and most often he summed it up laconically: "I have no idea what all this means…" Better to let people speculate, make inquiries, and raise their level of awareness, than to plunge into the deep waters of sacred iconography and its symbolism.

The Sanskrit root of the word "yantra" comes from "yam" — "instrument" or "support" — and "tra" comes from "trana," which means "release from bonds and fetters." A yantra is an instrument of meditation and contemplation. Briefly put, it is a device that helps one to attain spiritual liberation. A multi-purpose piece of training equipment, a guiding thread on the path to the highest reality. Hundreds of different yantras correspond to a great variety of deities, principles, and planets. Used in ceremonies and rituals, they may be drawn on metal plates, paper, or wood; they are made from flower petals, ashes, rice, or sand. In Vedic rituals a yantra is imprinted on a square copper, silver, or gold plate and then "charged" with mantras. The object is then "tuned" to a specific vibration of a deity and its energy, and so a connection is established. A "charged" yantra becomes a sacred object. Depending on its purpose, they may be made of mountain crystal or drawn on birch bark. Such yantras are usually used for peaceful and beneficent rituals. Gold, silver, or copper ones are meant to attract wealth. On iron, leather, or bone they are used for destructive and harmful rites. Sometimes the metal plate is coated with paste (of sandalwood, saffron, or aloe), and on it a sharp implement (of gold, wood, iron, or the thorn of a plant, depending on the aim of the ritual) is used to draw the required image. Three-dimensional yantras, made of precious metals, mountain crystal, coral, or lapis lazuli, foster the maintenance of peace and prosperity. The crucible in which the transformation of an herb, mineral, animal, or human soul takes place is set in motion by the energy of a specific mantra — and the yantra accumulates that energy until it begins to radiate of its own accord. Then the energy takes the form of the deity associated with the mantra.

Various images may be used, but by common acknowledgment the best yantra is the human body. Yet this complex tantric ritualism is important only for certain ends and tasks — chiefly oriented to the practical aspects of worldly life.

Here, on the smashana, the very notion of worldly success was an oxymoron. After they had been installed, the yantras began to produce strong vibrations, like a stout protective shield. But it seems they chiefly accumulated energy, like electrical capacitors — and stored it. What that energy is and what the consequences of its release might be remained a mystery.

The Shri Yantra worked like a magnet for the entire triumvirate of Indian feminine deities represented by the other yantras. But the queen of all yantras needed no mantras at all to radiate her power. Delivered by Babaji's silent companion on a December night in 1995, she was the first in this unique "collection."

After the initial excitement of Babaji's visit had subsided, he recalled the first mention of the Shri Yantra — the Aghori had spoken of it on the day before he left the body. Then, describing the ritual of summoning Parashurama, the ascetic had mentioned a yantra drawn in red powder directly upon the sand. Yet, for all the mystery of the subject, he had no idea what to do with the information he had received.

In the Shakta cult of Shri Vidya, the Shri Yantra has many meanings; the interlocking triangles of the yantra [footnote] Shri Vidya (in Sanskrit "knowledge," "learning," or "science") — a tantric religious system. In the Shakta theology of Shri Vidya the goddess is the supreme form, transcending the cosmos which is her manifestation. The system acknowledges and seeks to ensure both material well-being and self-realization. [/footnote] symbolize the cosmos, the human body, and the subtle aspects of non-duality. And yet he still needed pointers for a more practical understanding of the meaning of this yantra. Could this symbol of the Goddess somehow be tied to the place itself, to his own yogic path? At times, looking at the dark three-dimensional sculpture, it seemed to him as though the object had been made… in the future.

The next yantra was delivered in 1998. Someone simply left at the gate a heavy bag weighing about 20 kilograms. Also three-dimensional, the Rudra Yantra was made of bronze or copper, scrupulously turned on a lathe and disassemblable into separate parts. They decided to place it in the Shiva temple: installing the symbol of the fierce aspect of Mahadeva beside the Shivalingam seemed entirely fitting.

Another heavy parcel was delivered two months later. Ganganatha, with whom he had had the most interesting conversation earlier, came in person, bearing the yantras of Bagalamukhi and Dhumavati, which are usually used in potentially lethal and dangerous rituals. He left no instructions.

Examining the yantras, he recalled the case of one powerful tantrika known as Datia Maharaj. [footnote] Swami Ji Pitambar Pita (Datia Maharaj) — an ascetic who lived until his death in 1979 at the Pitambar Peeth Datia temple complex near Gwalior in Madhya Pradesh. The place is known as the stronghold of the most fearsome and ferocious tantric goddesses Bagalamukhi and Dhumavati. It is said that, as a devotee of the goddess Bagalamukhi, Datia Maharaj performed many miracles. In 1935 he installed the principal deity Bagalamukhi and observed penance until his death on June 2, 1979. He is also known and remembered for organizing a Brahma Yajna, a Vedic ritual conducted only during the times of the Mahabharata. This ritual was meant to stop the Sino-Indian war of 1962 and to save India from foreign invasion. The result was successful. Two months before his death Datia Maharaj installed the murti of Dhumavati. [/footnote] He had dared to install a statue of Bagalamukhi, after which he did not live even six months despite his supernatural powers and devotion: the might of the awakened Goddess proved too destructive for a mortal. These two Goddesses did not merely have power to be reckoned with — they were lethal. And he remained vigilant.

Another two years passed, and in January 2000 two visitors arrived at the smashana, but they were refused reception because of their connection with a highly unpleasant character. The visitors went to the shop of a local silversmith, where the pujari happened also to be present. One of these people said: "Give this to your guru," and without any explanation left a parcel. The yantras of Smashana Tara and Smashana Kali embodied fierce forms of Shakti and were installed in the house, but after about a week everyone began to feel a strong, slightly metallic smell, which soon filled the whole space. Nihar Mukherjee, a disciple from Bengal, identified the unknown odor as the smell of fresh blood. A heavy, oppressive energy settled in the house, and he turned to Shakti, praying for deliverance from any possible negative consequences. Evidently the prayers were heard, since the smell vanished.

In October of the following year, during the Navaratri celebrations, the Kamala Yantra arrived by what was by now a familiar method: an unknown person left it at the gate. Now the smashana was adorned with a unique combination of six yantras, representing six special forms of the Universal feminine energies.

The Divine Mother Adi Shakti is represented by ten cosmic personalities; She is revered as Mahakali, the fierce Goddess of time, life and death, rebirth and liberation. Devouring Time, She is reborn in Her dark formlessness.

The story of the origin of the Mahavidyas is rather curious: Shiva went off to a ritual without His first wife Sati, and She was both saddened and angered. After vain attempts to convince Shiva to allow Her to join Him, the enraged Sati turned into the Mahavidyas, surrounding Shiva on all ten sides of space. Wherever He went — north or south, east or west, whether He flew into the sky or tried to hide beneath the earth — they followed Him. Besides being various aspects of Shakti, the Mahavidyas also represent all the directions of space.

…To obtain the remaining four yantras took another nine years.

Early in the morning of June 6, 2009, he woke because Devahuti, in great excitement, was shaking him by the shoulders: "Get up quickly, we have to go there right now — I saw it in a dream!" She had dreamt of the ruins at the back of the campus; an imperious voice had said: "Go into the garden and unlock the room on the left side. Take everything you find." The room in question stood in a half-ruined building not far from the house — once a palace pavilion had stood there, of which only a one-story structure now remained.

It was almost dawn when they approached the building; the room had not been opened for nearly five years, the lock had rusted, and they had to call the pujari. He broke the lock with a crowbar, and they entered the premises. Right on the dusty floor stood four yantras, and beside them lay a small piece of bhojpatra (tree bark) on which was written in red ink a laconic message: it was no longer necessary to perform the purifying ritual, Prayashchitta, for new disciples. A substantial portion of their karmas was to be cleansed in the presence of the Guru. No other directions or explanations were in the note.

On the dusty floor they noticed a few footprints. Tiny prints, only 7 cm long, as if a dwarf had been stamping around the sacred objects, but beside them was another print — much larger, nearly 45 cm. How could they have appeared in a locked room? There was no one to answer.

Roughly the same size, the yantras looked as though they had been made by a single master. So Tripura Bhairavi, Chinnamasta, Matangi, and Bhuvaneshwari were reunited with the rest of the Mahavidyas.

Surrounded by a human ocean and yet hidden from curious eyes, the symbols of the Universal Mother now dwelt in the silence of the smashana, fed by the vibrations of millions of pilgrims circumambulating the Hill. Now the gigantic submarine of the Hill had a new and fully working control panel.

Matsyendranatha

results.english:
Recalling the events of that day, he could attest: the sharpest sensation had been the warmth of the immortal yogin's palm touching his head. He had wished to bow to the great Guru, but Matsyendranatha stopped him, tousling his head as one would a child, with a gesture of fatherly blessing. The touch was entirely human; the immortal's hand was undeniably of flesh and blood.

In the last week of April 2000, one of the Nathas who had visited Moksh Bhumi two years earlier walked through the gates. Ganganatha was carrying two yantras, tightly wrapped in cloth. The pujari who met him took the parcel and asked whether he wished to see Guru-ji, but Ganganatha refused: "Badda Natha se milne ka hukum nahi" — "I have not received the order to meet the Elder Natha…" And he added: "Tell the Yogiraj — he must arrive at Mahurgad on June 16, at noon. Let him approach the head priest of Dattatreya's temple and ask permission to meditate in a secluded place. Matsyendranatha will meet him there."

This might have sounded like a hoax, were it not for the inquiries he had made about the three Nathas, and were it not that he knew that Ganganatha — wiry, smiling, with even slightly yellowish teeth, a man of about 38 to 40 — was one of the immortals. Possessed of hints and vague allegories, he had to make decisions based on the strangest truths, brushing aside logical conclusions and so-called "common sense." Sometimes logic only got in the way.

The founder of the Natha tradition, Matsyendranatha, is an immortal Siddha who learned the secret of yoga from Shiva Himself. Historians of the Natha tradition are almost certain that the cult itself arose in the 10th century, though many references point to a much earlier presence of Matsyendranatha on the Indian spiritual landscape.

…It was said that the boy was born under an unlucky star, and the astrologer somehow persuaded his parents to throw him into Lake Sheshnag in the mountains of Amarnath. Obstacles were often removed by such radical means before misfortune could overtake the family. The well-being of the clan was more important than the probable scenarios and prospects of a new life that had hardly begun. The infant was swallowed by a fish, and grew up inside its belly.

One day the fish came to lie on the bottom of the lake where Shiva was teaching the secrets of yoga to His wife Parvati. The Goddess dozed off. And to Shiva's question, "Are you listening, dear?", the young Matsyendranatha answered in the affirmative. Thus he learned the secrets of yoga and began performing sadhana inside the fish's womb. After twelve years Matsyendra emerged into the light an enlightened Siddha, and the whole adventure was reflected in his name — "Lord of Fish."

Other legends maintain that originally the fish itself heard the secret instructions on yoga — and became enlightened. Shiva noticed this, and out of compassion sprinkled it with water; the "baptism" was followed by an instantaneous transformation, and the fish became a human being.

The first direct disciple of Shiva, who passed yoga on to mortals, Matsyendranatha stands apart, as the most outstanding figure among other worthy yogins. In the tradition of the Mahasiddhas He is honored as the Teacher of the other great founder of the Natha Sampradaya, Gorakhnath. Adepts call Him Dada Matsyendra Natha — "Grandfather-Guru." All Matsyendranatha had to do was to give the world one outstanding disciple and simply watch over the triumphs of Gorakhnath.

Some legends speak of Matsyendranatha's ability to leave the body at will and to assume other forms, remaining in them for long periods. In essence, he continues to pass from one body into another, and this skill makes Him immortal. Yet for a great Siddha eternal life is not the goal but only a side-effect of yoga. Having attained immortality, one must know why and how to use this priceless gift — herein lies the task of a highly developed mind.

And now this immortal was inviting him to a meeting in Mahurgad. Even if it was a mystification, mystifications of this caliber were within the powers only of supreme beings. The invitation had to be accepted; to ignore it would have been reckless and disrespectful. On June 15, with his disciple Rajendra Sharma, they took the train from Agra to Nanded, where they spent an unremarkable night in a hotel. Early in the morning they were already in the bus that bounced over the potholes through the mountainous landscapes of Maharashtra into Mahurgad. After about three hours the famous mountain ridges opened before them, here and there crowned with temples; one was dedicated to Renuka, the mother of Parashurama, on another stood the temple of Lord Dattatreya and His mother Anasuya.

The most mysterious deity of the Indian pantheon, Dattatreya is considered the incarnation, or Avatar, of all three Hindu gods — Brahma, Vishnu, and Shiva, together known as the Trinity, the Trimurti. Datta Guru is revered by the Nathas as the First teacher of yoga, and this in no way contradicts one of the titles of Shiva, who is also called Adi Guru.

Arriving in Mahurgad about 11 in the morning, they went up the hill to the temple of Dattatreya. After a brief darshan, he told the priest he wished to see "Dattatreya Yogi" — the honorific title of the mahant of the temple, which was managed by Aghoris of the Ananda Margis sect. The pandit led him to the back of the temple and opened the door of one of the rooms. Greeting the orange-robed abbot, he introduced himself as a yogin from Govardhan and presented him with a copy of the Bhagavad Gita, explaining that he was the author of the yogic commentaries on the sacred book. The mahant was very well-disposed toward him. The sect of the Ananda Margis revered Sri Krishna, and pilgrims from Braj were always warmly received.

When the exchange of courtesies was finished, he asked the abbot a favor: might one find a secluded spot to meditate, somewhere on the temple grounds? The attendant led him to the back courtyard, to a small structure perched above a high precipice. There the escort politely but firmly woke the two pilgrims who were sleeping inside: "The Maharaj of Govardhan is going to meditate here."

The two tiny dusty rooms had no furniture, but none of this mattered; the sole criterion was seclusion. Sitting on the floor, he tried to slow his pounding heart. About ten minutes later an unusually tall, slightly stooped man entered the room, accompanied by two women.

Those who have had the chance to meet gods or immortals know the feeling in which nervousness is mingled with fear. There is no "correct" line of conduct to choose, since etiquette for dealing with higher beings simply does not exist. He tried to half-rise to bow, but the visitor stopped him with a gesture and tousled his head as one would a child. For some reason it was precisely this gesture that at once and irrevocably convinced him of the reality of what was happening. Matsyendranatha said gently: "No, no, relax — all yogins are equal, you do not need to bow…" No one had ever spoken to him with such warmth.

The Natha sat before him on the dusty floor. The only article of his clothing was a loincloth of white cloth; he was light-skinned and rather slender, with black hair cut just below the ears, and a short-trimmed beard. The little room was so small that even seated the yogin seemed tall. Two tall, stately, very beautiful women with long black hair tied up in knots on the crown of their heads sat down behind him. Dressed in dhotis of brick color, such as sadhvis usually wear, the women-ascetics smiled silently. Matsyendranatha did not introduce them, and he did not dare to ask who they were. All three had dark kundala-earrings in their earlobes.

Matsyendranatha spoke of matters connected with the meaning and consequences of installing the Shivalingam on the smashana, confirming Ganganatha's words: for centuries no one had ever successfully completed this ritual, and those who dared had not survived. Outwardly nothing in the act itself was difficult, and the mere placement of a stone at the cremation site of the Raja presented no technical challenge. But for the mystical ritual to set in motion irreversible processes, fateful for entire nations and states, many factors had to come together. The Nathas regarded this event as the summoning of the Avadhuta, Aghora — the fifth wrathful form of Shiva. The installation of the Shivalingam on the Smashana marked the beginning of a new count of the cycle of Creation.

Matsyendranatha instructed him to wear large round kundalas — the same earrings that pierced his own earlobes. This symbol of yogic discipline was obligatory for anyone who lived permanently on the smashana and conducted yogic sadhana there. The Guru of the Nathas explained in detail the particulars of the dhuni, the yogic ritual fire, naming an auspicious time to begin the rite. The aspects of laying the dhuni and the rite itself contained a multitude of technical details, and he tried not to miss a word.

It should be noted that at some moment he felt the presence of Devahuti, though physically this was impossible; only he and the three Nathas were sitting in the tiny room above the precipice… And yet it seemed to him that Devahuti was standing right behind his back.

The meeting was dense though brief; not half an hour passed before Matsyendranatha said: "We are leaving; you too may go…" The Nathas rose together (he hurriedly sprang up) and one after another walked out, bending their heads under the low doorframe. After a moment's hesitation he looked out of the room, but the courtyard was already empty, and only the orange flags shifted slightly above the sharp roofs of the temple in the white-hot sky.

Returning into the room for his travel-bag, he noticed only his own footprints on the dusty floor. Where the Nathas had been seated there were no prints at all, and one could only guess whether they had been in etheric bodies or simply left no marks because of the different structure of their physical sheaths… Everything was possible. According to the Yoga-Sutras, those who have attained control of udana-vayu, [footnote] Udana-vayu — one of the five pranas (vayus), residing chiefly in the hands, feet, and head. [/footnote] a certain type of prana, leave no traces.

Everything was utterly ordinary, mundane; the noon was becoming agonizingly hot, the temple had emptied. Rajendra was waiting for him in front of the entrance to the temple complex, and he asked whether anyone had come out of the courtyard; his companion had seen no one. He remembered with piercing clarity the sound of Matsyendranatha's voice, the look in His eyes, the touch of His hand — all of it was real, unquestionable, and at the same time like a mirage. But the instructions dictated to him were entirely concrete.

Why had he been summoned precisely to Mahurgad? The meeting took place around noon, and that might mean that Dattatreya Himself was making His usual midday halt there, and Matsyendranatha with his retinue was following the Guru. Sometimes Guru Datta could take the guise of an old beggar; appearances are deceiving when divine play is involved. Practically anyone may turn out to be an all-powerful Deity in disguise — asking alms, granting blessings, or capable of cursing. Therefore vigilance, caution, and courtesy must be developed by all, not only by yogins… Every encounter was a test.

Dhuni

results.english:
The laying of the dhuni began at once, following the instructions received at Mahurgad. The preparation took nearly a year, since certain technical aspects demanded high precision. The foundation had to be of a prescribed size and shape; specific material and secret ingredients were required. Three rows of low stepped tiers were laid above a particular yantra, in accordance with a specific positioning of the stars and time. In order to keep the peculiarities of the construction secret, the necessary components were ordered from different places and workshops. Not that anyone could try to build a copy of the dhuni, but the precautions were not superfluous. The dhuni was too powerful an instrument of communication with the subtle worlds, and its secrets had to be handled with care.

In September 2001 the order of the ritual changed: now, before the puja itself, he kindled the dhuni; the pujari blew a horn, and the pure sound merged with the inaudible echo of the long-vanished yogic horns of countless past adepts. The sound summoned the invisible, confirming the unbroken primordial bond between the causal and the astral, the gross and the subtle, between the night of the mind and the noon of the heart.

The yogic dhuni differs from other fires. Wandering sadhus usually carry the fire from a dhuni with them, kindling it only for worship — not for cooking; this is part of asceticism. But the fire of the smashana never travels. Its flame may be used only for the purpose of cremation; it opens a door into another dimension. A sacred depression in the earth, the yogic dhuni symbolizes the yoni, the birth-organ of Shakti. It is a place for the worship of the Mother. One of the ten Mahavidyas, the goddess Dhumavati, is directly linked to the dhuni and its powers. Even her name, "the smoky one," indicates this link. According to legend, the all-consuming hunger that overcame the Goddess made Her devour Her husband Shiva while He sat in deep meditation, but He came out of every pore of Her body, like smoke.

"Perceived as Void, a dissolved form of consciousness, when all beings are sunk in the sleep of the highest Reality, having devoured the entire universe, the seer-poets call her the most glorious and elder, Dhumavati. She dwells in the various forms of dreams, lost memory, fantasies and the misunderstanding of beings immersed in the illusion of the world; but among yogins she becomes the force destroying all thought, the undoubted Samadhi (death and liberation from it)." (Ganapati Muni, Uma Sahasram 38)

When the foundation of the dhuni was being laid, the Dhumavati yantra brought by Ganganatha was already on the smashana, and with the consecration of the dhuni the Smoky Goddess Herself welcomed, through the yogic flame, the myriad ancient spirits — mostly bound to Shiva — entering this world. Her womb lay deep in the earth, but it was not She who gave birth to these spirits. Her yoni was only a tunnel from another realm, providing entry into this world for various beings. Each night the door between worlds opened anew; the missions and motives of the arriving spirits were most diverse. The majority were Shivaganas, the servants of Lord Shiva, members of His retinue. Bhutas, [footnote] Bhuta — a ghost; also a name for the basic elements and a state of existence. [/footnote] nagas, yakshas, pramatis, pishachas, raksha-ganas, vinayakas, guhyakas, manushyas, and vidyadharas — legions of phantoms arrived each night and varied from transparent spheres to melting, indistinct triangles and squares, from green, playfully fluttering bird-like shapes to dark whirls with a rainbow sheen.

In the material world consciousness could assume various forms, depending on the status of its bearer. To enter here through the portal at the cremation site, beside the place of Shiva worship, was an honor earned by these spirits through centuries of austerities. There were no human ghosts among them, and therefore they did not crave bodily incarnation as the homeless and grieving human souls do. The purposes of their coming remained a mystery. Quite possibly the smashana was for them a railway station, a transfer point. It was also the dwelling of their Lord.

…Once the goddess Uma asked Shiva why He preferred cremation grounds, filled with corpses and vultures, demons and jackals, when there are so many beautiful places in the world. Maheshvara replied that He had wandered the world in search of a fitting, clean place for meditation and could not find a single one. In despair He created various demons, but His terrifying entourage could only dwell on smashanas. Yet He had grown accustomed to their company and did not wish to wander without His retinue, and so He decided to remain at the cremation grounds. In His presence the spirits did not harm people. It is held that the Shivaganas, His servants, guard Shiva and do not admit the unworthy. Only true devotees, who have defied death and are free of passion and fear, can be near the "Lord of Ghosts," as Shiva is called.

Before he began to live on the smashana, such legends sounded entertaining; now he saw these beings every night. Watching their arrival, he felt like a doorman.

When the dhuni-fire, fed for the first time on sacrificial ghee, flared up, a great ultraviolet sphere shot out of the leaping flames. For some moments it hovered above the fire, then rose higher and dove downward, as if bowing in greeting. It was about 11 in the evening. When he returned to the house, the television, which was on, was broadcasting the moment of the destruction of the World Trade Center towers in New York. Could this terrible event somehow be linked with the kindling of a yogic fire somewhere in northern India? Large-scale tragedies usually occur under the influence of hidden forces and mark the beginning of new cycles in human society. Realists and pragmatists tend to ignore ambiguous connections, but the yogic mind sees beyond the three-dimensionality of past, present, and future. The mighty symbols of capitalism's permanence — the skyscrapers — were destroyed with the merciless intent to deprive America of its belief in its own invulnerability and superiority. September 11th became the beginning of a redistribution of the world's balance of power, the reshuffling of an unseen deck. But only for humanity; as always, the gods and immortals watched. For them the great events of human history are mere indicators of emerging trends. Long before the events themselves the dwellers of higher spheres already knew the seeds of causes planted by the Gardener-Time. The gods did not experiment with humanity out of cruelty or short-sightedness; they played their heavenly games for pleasure and amusement, since they had all the Time in the world. And these games lasted far longer than any so-called civilizations. The planet was the gods' sandbox, where on the sites of ruined castles and cities new ones were inevitably built.

What was happening beyond the gates of the smashana was no more real than what surrounded him here, on the Land of Liberation. Events in the world of men are marked by the predictability and limitedness of the meager historical experience of humanity. The kingdom of spirits is far more ancient, and the life-cycles of astral beings are non-linear and complex.

…The tongues of flame danced before him, constantly changing shape; the fantastic beings bursting out of the abyss were to take part in a new game. The immortals who had ordered the portal of the dhuni opened could see the gleam of this flame from wherever they were.

Accessories of Sadhana

The first pair of kundalas was cut from the very thick hide of a black rhinoceros, taken from an ancient Rajasthani shield-dhala. [footnote] Dhala (or dhaal) — a round shield up to 60 cm in diameter; it could be flat or curved. Such shields were widespread from India to Persia and were made of animal hides or metal (sometimes wootz steel). [/footnote] Adorned with golden mounts, the earrings were an exact copy of those Matsyendranatha wore, who had said to him during the meeting in Mahurgad: "You will soon begin a special ritual, and it will become part of your life. Without kundalas made of rhinoceros horn or hide, no one can endure such discipline."

Similar earrings were occasionally found in ancient burials, and he always hoped that the finds were chance, since the deliberate desecration of yogic samadhi-burials promised nothing but misfortune for those who dared disturb the bodies of ascetics.

Among other factors, rhinoceros horn was preferred for its durability. In antiquity the ascetics used the powdered hide of this animal as an antiseptic for wounds, and the rhinoceros itself was considered pious, nearly sacred — something like a magical unicorn. He was the vahana-mount of the goddess Dhavdi, and was honored for the secluded mode of life and the stoicism associated with the hermit's way. The same way that yogins lived.

To obtain rhinoceros horn was practically impossible; these animals topped the list of protected species. Even black-market dealers undertook the search reluctantly. Yet luck smiled on them, and one of his disciples, Mr. Verma of Gwalior, managed to find an antique shield. To use the darkened hard rhinoceros hide of a shield once belonging to a Rajput warrior for yogic accessories seemed entirely fitting.

His friend and disciple Praveen Bharadwaj pierced his earlobes right in the darshan hall. Not being sure of the outcome of the procedure, he had asked a few disciples to join in for company. Surrounded by a support group, he had to set the example and had no right to show hesitation. The pain was quite slight, but the sound of the needle passing through the lobe of his ear seemed to him disgusting. At first he was acutely aware that great heavy earrings were now constantly hanging in his ears, and noticed the impression they made on visitors. But the practical and mystical effect of the kundalas began to be felt at once.

Two fine channels, called Pusha [footnote] Pusha Nadi — the "nourishing energy channel" running from the great toe of the left foot to the right ear. [/footnote] and Yashaswini [footnote] Yashaswini Nadi — the "energy channel of splendor," issuing from the great toe of the right foot and ending at the left ear. [/footnote] Nadi, ensure the unhindered flow of prana. Beginning in the earlobes, they cross diagonally through the entire body, ending in the great toes. The weight of the kundalas stimulated these channels, "closing the circuit" and redirecting pranic energy. The change in the flow of energy refined his yogic practice, raising it to an optimal level. Pranayamas became perfect, since Prana stimulated the "opening" of Sushumna, [footnote] Sushumna — the chief energy channel of the subtle body. The life-force energy, Prana, travels along channels called nadis. Although in the subtle body (pranamaya kosha) there are 72,000 such channels, only three are principal: Ida, Pingala, and Sushumna, corresponding to the left, right, and central lines of the body. Sushumna is the most important nadi, passing through the central axis of the body, through the spinal cord. The kind of energy that flows through the Sushumna nadi is saturated with the pure creative force called Kundalini. If pollution or blockages obstruct other nadis of the body, Prana cannot flow through Sushumna. Such blockage leads to mental and physical imbalance and prevents the Kundalini energy from entering the main channel. [/footnote] — and to feel, to observe, to experiment with this flow was a genuine delight.

A tightrope walker carries a bamboo pole for balance — and the seemingly uneventful life on the smashana also resembled walking a tightrope. It was necessary to keep one's inner equilibrium, to level the emotional waves, and the kundalas played the role of the balancing pole. Or of a lightning rod, redirecting the mighty energies with which yogic sadhana, the rituals of worship, and the ancient place itself constantly charged mind and body. Plainly speaking, the earrings were necessary to maintain…
