teachings.teaching: The fire of the smashana never travels — its flame may serve only cremation; it is a door into another dimension.
teachings.lens_note: Unlike wandering sadhus who carry the dhuni-fire, the smashana fire is fixed; it is itself a portal, not a tool of mobility.

teachings.teaching: Shiva dwells on cremation grounds because no clean place could be found in the world for His retinue of demons — but in His presence the spirits do not harm people; only those free of passion and fear may stand near Him.
teachings.lens_note: The smashana is not a 'haunted' place; it is the only place that can hold the entire spectrum from Mahadeva to His most fearsome ganas without bleeding into the human world.

teachings.teaching: Large historical events (e.g. September 11) are echoes of subtle-world rearrangements visible to immortals long beforehand — humanity sees only the surface; the gods see seeds planted by the Gardener-Time.
teachings.lens_note: The yogic mind sees beyond the three-dimensionality of past-present-future; the planet is the gods' sandbox.

teachings.teaching: The weight of kundalas on the earlobes closes the Pusha–Yashaswini circuit, redirects pranic flow, and stimulates the opening of Sushumna — so pranayamas become perfect.
teachings.lens_note: The earring is not ornament but a physical-energetic instrument; without it the discipline of the smashana cannot be sustained.

results.english:
…mental equilibrium.

…The symbols of belonging to the Natha tradition — the kundalas — are traditionally put on in the final stages of initiation into the order. Usually a specially trained guru cuts with a razor or a double-bladed knife the central parts of the cartilage of the ear-shells and inserts wooden sticks of neem into the openings. When the wounds heal, the earrings are inserted into the holes. Novices wear kundalas of plastic; in former times they were made of clay or even of glass. Some Nathas wear gold kundalas, and the weight is borne by a cord thrown over the head. The material of the earrings changes as the Natha-yogin advances in the practice. Usually the earrings are either flat or cylindrical. The former are called "darshan," the latter "kundal." With them fate marks those who have chosen the path of asceticism and faith.

A Natha who has received the kundalas may become a Guru and teach others. With practice and discipline the inner force called kundali or darshani develops, grows, and remains with the ascetic throughout his journey — and that is why some of the elder Nathas speak of an inner wearing of the kundalas. The yogin is born anew, in a transcendent sense — the earrings protect their bearer from everything corruptible and subject to decay. The yogin becomes "one with the Absolute," and removing the kundalas is tantamount to renouncing the highest grace. Some Nathas still remember that the piercing of the ears and the wearing of kundalas activates the nadi-channels and improves the pranayamas. Those whom he had met spoke of acquiring certain abilities by means of the kundalas. There was also the widespread opinion that those who wear them become immortal.

Having seen Matsyendranatha's kundalas, he no longer disputed these claims. Incidentally, the legendary Guru of the Nathas wore his kundalas in the earlobes, and not in the middle cartilages as modern Nathas do. Perhaps this is why such ascetics were nicknamed "kanphata" — "split-ears." And yet many ancient statues of yogins were made with earrings hanging from the lobes, in the old style.

He had no formal initiation into the line of the Nathas, though that did not prevent Ganganatha from addressing him as "Elder Natha."

Sometimes he tried to arrange all events in chronological order, but memory always strayed from the course; still, certain facts of his life seemed to determine further events on the smashana. The steady state of controlled samadhi, the installation of the Shivalingam and the worship of Shiva, the strict sadhana, and the inherited responsibility of teaching others — all this was the purest and strictest yoga, corresponding to the principles of Natha discipline regardless of formal initiation. To be frank, he had always considered yoga a monolith, the indivisible philosophy of the transformation of spirit and body — and what particular followers called themselves was not essential. Yes, the line of succession was important. But faithfulness to discipline was even more important.

Time passed, and the hide of the kundalas faded, becoming brownish. In 2010 one of his disciples from Germany bought at auction an antique Chinese cup made of rhinoceros horn, and from it new kundalas were carved. The pranayamas became still more intense; the practice acquired depth, becoming more refined. Receiving compliments on the new earrings, he smiled slightly: "No kundalas — no Kundalini." The students took his words quite seriously, and many began piercing their ears.

Shiva and Shakti, dhuni and the Avadhuta, kundalas and Kundalini… The coiled Kundalini and the cycle of Samsara, the circular motions of Time, the endless renewal and alchemy of Creation — he was well aware of all this symbolism, since even Vedic rituals are compared to "the serpent biting its own tail." The alchemical link between the physical and the metaphysical, between place and myth, the invisible filaments between human destinies and the gods, stood out clearly here on the smashana. The combination of these factors yielded important, sometimes inexplicable results. All of this too was yoga — the joining of different parts, factors, efforts, and conditions into one.

The heavy bracelet around his wrist was made of an alloy of gold and silver and depicted a cobra with a pair of ruby eyes. The serpent was biting its own tail. The bracelet drew the attention of visitors, and he was constantly asked about its symbolism. He would answer that it was simply an ornament, and only rarely went into details.

The bracelet was a symbol of Time, the ring of Ouroboros. [footnote] Ouroboros — an ancient symbol depicting a snake (sometimes a dragon) biting its tail. In alchemy it is a symbol of gnosticism and hermeticism. The term itself comes from the ancient Greek for "tail-eating," and the symbol is interpreted as eternal cyclic renewal or the cycle of life, death, and rebirth, just as the shedding of skin by the snake symbolizes the transmigration of souls. In the Indian tradition this symbolism is used to describe Kundalini. According to the medieval "Yoga-Kundalini Upanishad," "the divine force, Kundalini, shines like the stalk of a young lotus; like a serpent coiled in rings, she holds her tail in her mouth and lies in a half-sleeping state at the base of the body." [/footnote] Some Nathas wore similar serpent bracelets, but this one depicted a king cobra with its hood spread. The serpent unfurls it only when preparing to strike… The coils of the curled Kundalini correspond to the cycle of samsara, whose recurring movements reflect the eternal pulsation of Time.

The symbolism of the ornament was layered, and he liked that. The bracelet symbolized renewal, immortality, and the Alchemy of Creation. The Ouroboros could fertilize itself, and also kill itself and return to life again. The serpent was the symbol of non-dual unity, the secret of prima Materia [footnote] Prima Materia — in alchemy and philosophy, prima materia, materia prima, or first matter is the ubiquitous original substance required for the alchemical "magnum opus" and the creation of the philosopher's stone. It is described as the primitive formless basis of all matter, comparable to chaos, the quintessence, or aether. [/footnote] — first matter. Alchemy itself, undoubtedly, arose from the merging of conscious and unconscious, of masculine and feminine, of heaven and earth, of the body and the microcosm; it lived in everything and contained within itself the potential of myriad emotions. For Spirit there was nothing more beautiful than endlessly to conceive, to bring forth, and to know itself anew — with joy and trembling.

Once, taking off the bracelet before puja, he thought fleetingly of it as a symbol of the unconscious bitten by its twin — the logical mind, trying to awaken its brother. Both of them were children of Universal Thought, the mother of Creation.

Aghora, "the Most Beautiful"

His own practice he preferred to call Smashana-sadhana. It is known that some tantrikas and black magicians perform rituals at cremation grounds for the attainment of particular results: power, material wealth, or control over others (whether spirits or living beings). His own goals and tasks were entirely different: the practice of the higher Yoga, the worship of the highest Teacher and first Guru, Shiva, along with Babaji and other teachers of the parampara. He performed sadhana, guiding and supporting those who asked for help or knowledge, in the name of the inner Spirit and the Universal Mother, honoring and serving the land that had sheltered him and all the others.

The manifesto was short but pithy, and time only polished and sharpened the methods and means of realizing these aims. Nevertheless, in the eyes of the broad public, life at a cremation ground was inconceivable, since too many frightening stories and legends were tied to the smashana. The lay person could not understand why anyone would voluntarily settle on a smashana and remain there for many years without leaving. It did not fit the frames of the generally accepted cultural codes and algorithms of society. The only people who lived permanently on cremation grounds were the Aghoris. Perhaps that is why, when one of the members of ISKCON called him Aghori Baba, the nickname stuck for a while. Indeed, this label made the situation more or less comprehensible to lay people. Come to think of it, the name suited him quite well: he lived on the smashana without leaving, wore a black tilak [footnote] Tilak — a particular mark on the forehead, usually made with sandalwood paste. [/footnote] on his forehead — the mark of an Aghori — and conducted the puja to Shiva at night. In addition, he practiced some strange and incomprehensible yoga, so far removed from the simple physical exercises popular in "yogic" ashrams.

Needless to say, the unique brand arose by itself; he never even tried to impress anyone. Dressed in red robes, adorned with massive necklaces of sacred rudraksha, with the heavy black earrings, he blew the horn before the dhuni against the backdrop of fabled palaces, making the offering to Bhairava, the fierce form of Shiva, surrounded by formidable black dogs. All this could startle the imagination of ordinary townsfolk and foreigners alike. But ordinary people, so to speak, visited him rarely. Most understood that he was a yogin and a Guru, and the other associations were secondary and mostly unfounded.

Paying no heed to the rumors, he rarely denied the tales of his connection with the path of Aghora. There was neither strength nor time for it, since the first years on the Smashana were given exclusively to survival. He and Devahuti had had to hold their ground. So he was not interested in the epithets the locals showered upon him, nor in the gossip they kept chewing over. Only after years did the events and the strange links among their participants begin to yield to any analysis.

…The very name "Aghor" had ancient origin. One of the five faces of Shiva is known as Aghor, the most powerful aspect of Mahadeva. He is the one who dwells on the smashana, at the entrance to infinite reality, on the edge of the Void, on the rim of Time. The "Shiva Purana" — one of the most ancient texts — contains a hymn composed by the chief of the clan of celestial singers, the Gandharvas, the "Shiva Mahimna Stotram":

"Aghoranna paro mantro
Nasti tattvam Guru param."

"The name of Aghora is the mantra surpassing all mantras.
There is nothing higher than knowing the true nature of the Guru."

The tradition of Aghora (like that of the Nathas and the Kapalikas) originated with Dattatreya. In time this Deity inspired many monastic movements in Shaivism, Vaishnavism, and Shaktism. Strictly speaking, all the members of these cults were engaged in one and the same thing: cognition of the nature of Shiva — that is, the nature of Time itself.

Both paths, Tantra and Aghora, arose in the pre-Vedic era; they should not be linked exclusively with "darkness" or "light." Tantra in its ordinary, utilitarian interpretation was only a technique that anyone could study and use who possessed sufficient determination, perseverance, and a pure heart (perhaps the most difficult and most rarely fulfilled condition). Not all Tantra was Aghora, but all Aghora was permeated with the spirit of Tantra.

For the Aghori, everything connected with death and the passage into another state of consciousness has a sacred significance; they enter total darkness in order to see the light of self-realization. Holding this approach to be the most effective, the Aghoris perform somber rituals surrounded by death and decay. For example, in shava-sadhana a corpse is used as an altar for summoning Smashana Tara.

Some black magicians and tantrikas call themselves Aghori for the sake of intimidation or to attract clientele. Such people visit the smashanas to catch and control certain spirits by means of tantric rituals, which make extensive use of alcohol and meat, since it is known that dark spirits are drawn to intoxicating substances. That is why the tantrikas drink whisky or moonshine, so that the spirit may contact them directly or even enter them, "possessing" the body. In certain rituals, sitting in a prescribed manner upon the corpse of a girl or a man possessing particular physical qualities and reciting a secret mantra, one can summon this or that goddess or a powerful astral being who can grant the initiator's desires. Sometimes the tantrikas may take these beings into "slavery" and compel them to serve. This is not…

results.english:
…the path of Aghora. To obtain material gains by enslaving astral inhabitants is primitive sorcery, not the path to liberation.

The cult of Aghora has been greatly transformed over the last hundred years. Practices traditionally condemned from the standpoint of social morality have become less radical. Still, Aghora insists on a balanced personal practice of the adept. It must include both sadhana and seva — selfless service. The Aghoris often heal sufferers through purification, and this is the main message of the rituals they perform.

It is believed that Aghoris are capable of removing illness in a process of "transforming healing." However, the impurity must be transferred to another person, and in some cases the successful healing requires the sacrifice of an animal. Some call this process the transfer of karma. But such rites, and those who can perform them with guaranteed result, are extremely rare. Permanent residence on a smashana is considered impossible because of the effect on the human psyche of otherworldly (in the literal sense) forces. In places where portals into other dimensions open and the line between worlds is thin, something out of the ordinary always happens. As a rule, possession by evil spirits, as well as sudden and strange occurrences, happen there without any visible cause. Sattvic, pure worship at a smashana is considered impossible, since Aghora is associated with the dark side of reality. People link death with the frightening and the unknown. Therefore it is held that to remain pure at a place of cremation, beside death and darkness, is impossible. Stereotypes paint the image of a ragged, dishevelled, dull-eyed and stupefied ascetic using a human skull as a cup.

He, who had never in his life smoked, was a teetotaler and a vegetarian, performed daily the rituals considered sattvic — "pure." It is supposed that any stimulants which directly affect the mind weaken the sadhana. In a state of intoxication the mind clouds, the capacity to make decisions weakens, and clear understanding of the true nature of things blurs or is blocked. Many times he had watched spirits "attach themselves" to those who had fallen into dependence on any intoxicants, be they drugs, good old alcohol, sex, money, or power — the mightiest of all temptations and trials.

Yes, in the broadest sense he was an Aghori, because he lived permanently at the place of cremation. But if it came to it, he was the only "sattvic" Aghori, even though he did not follow the traditions of the order. He knew of their rituals, had heard many stories, and had associated with those who called themselves followers of the cult of Aghora. Some of these people belonged to known lines and orders; others were simply impostors. Conversation with the true Aghoris was refined and interesting, unlike the sour, stale, and dismal energy of the swindlers practicing the lowest sort of black magic.

In the twentieth year of his life at Govardhan he understood that in all of India there were probably no more than five or six true Aghoris. And to find them was incredibly hard, since they did not wish to be found. Apparently the man whom he had met in Benares at the insistence of Satyacharan Lahiri was one of these rare ascetics.

True followers of Aghora live on smashanas for the single purpose of being closer to the Gods, since the cremation ground is considered the home of Shiva and Shakti. There they must coexist with a multitude of other, not the most peaceful and not at all benevolent beings of Mahadeva's retinue. The overcoming of fear means liberation, and liberated consciousness is ready to receive the Divine. So far as he could judge, the immortals themselves were fearless. For them, lacking the fear of death, there was nothing terrible in Creation; there was only beauty. In a world originally beautiful by its very manifestation, there could be no ugliness or impurity; life and death are sides of the same shining coin.

From time to time, when he called yoga the prelude to Aghora, he met uncomprehending looks. Of course yoga led the adept step by step through the stages of evolution of the mind, but why did it precede Aghora?

Its very essence was "pure beauty," not "darkness." The full opposite of horror and ugliness. "Ghora" meant "the concentration of darkness," and "A-ghora" — dazzling. When the dormant subconscious opens, it overcomes the state of sleep.

The state of Aghora is the opposite of the dark inertia of a sleeping mind. For him, it was one of the highest stages of the soul's development, not a sectarian creed or cult. As shocking as this may sound to some, he considered Kriya yoga the preparation for true Aghora, because yoga helps one to set foot on the path of knowing the dazzling Light, to embrace the state of non-Darkness, to act with absolute meaningfulness and purity in the most difficult situations, and to remain in constant awareness of Time.

In his faith there was a personal element as well: he had learned from Mahavatar himself that an advanced practitioner can attain the "stage of Aghora" only at a place of cremation. During the memorable meeting, Babaji declared: those who have passed through and untied certain "knots" in the subtle channels are awaited by the smashana. The Guru of the parampara said literally the following: "The sadhak ready for the passing of the Muladhara Granthi Bheda can do this only at the place of cremation." That is why fate had brought him here.

At various stages of the practice of kriya the student "unties" energy knots and removes the "locks" sealing the centers of energy. By means of special pranayamas the yogin "passes through" these stages. The passing of the Muladhara (also called the "passing of the Knot of Rudra") is the highest stage. A practitioner who has succeeded in approaching this level is destined to be drawn by the smashana; this place will be "allotted" to him so that he may advance further. It is impossible simply to come and settle at a place of cremation; only the mighty undercurrents of destiny can bring a yogin to such a destination. Besides luck and destiny, one more component is obligatory: the grace of the First Guru of yoga. Only Shiva Himself can permit anyone to remain in His home for an indefinite period.

Psychedelic Flowers, Divine Trees, Astral and Other Creatures

For experiments with mercury he required a rather rare ingredient — the juice of the flower of black datura. Both this "moon flower" (or "devil's trumpets") and its seeds and flowers were potentially psychoactive. Mercury boiled down and "evaporated" in this juice acquired certain qualities, and he wanted to see the results. An old friend, the Ayurvedic doctor Vaid-ji, learned of a sadhu from the neighboring village of Barsana who had this plant. Wishing to see the results of the experiment with mercury, Vaid-ji went to Barsana hoping to buy datura, but returned empty-handed and furious. "That bastard refused even to share the seeds!!!" The doctor steamed for quite a long while, continuing to award the stingy sadhu the choicest epithets.

The monsoon came, and when the rains ended the whole locality suddenly grew thick with black datura. Early in the morning and at night hundreds of flowers released a stupefying fragrance; people, dogs, and cows fell into a semi-comatose state until the hot midday sun evaporated the intoxicating fog. Fortunately it did not cause delirium or hallucinations; the mind simply slowed down, sinking into dreams, and the contours of reality blurred. No one protested; he personally liked the scent, and the effect of the datura assisted a gentle entry into the night sadhana.

From time to time the rains brought not only the needed moisture but new inhabitants as well. Once, with a downpour, hundreds of tiny frogs rained down upon the earth, enthusiastically welcomed by snakes and birds. Another thunderstorm delivered myriad worms, providing an abundant feast for the feathered and scaly inhabitants of the smashana. The surviving worms loosened the soil, making it more porous and fertile. The apex of the heavenly gifts was a medium-sized brown water turtle, which flopped down to the earth right in front of them during another storm. The pujari went to check on it: shocked by the flight and the fall, it lay motionless, and they thought it was dead. But after half an hour leathery limbs poked out from under the shell, and the airfarer slowly crawled away.

Rare plants, exotic creatures, and ghosts continued to appear regularly, as though someone were staging a play that maintained an unending intrigue. In the summer of 1997 hundreds of tender shoots of the red river fig-tree, gular, suddenly began to push insistently through the hard ground.

Once an acquaintance from New York brought along a guest, a connoisseur of Vastu Shastra [footnote] Vastu Shastra — traditional ancient Indian science of architecture and spatial design. [/footnote] and a tantrika. For seven days this man silently sipped tea, occasionally taking short walks through the campus. On the seventh day the visitor bowed before him: "The great grace of Dattatreya is with you; these fig trees are directly connected with Him. Being extremely finicky, they rarely survive when planted, but your land was chosen by them itself…" The trees grew quickly, and by the time of Dattatreya's arrival their crowns were already shading part of the garden. And, on the eve of the coming of Guru Datta, His emissaries and security service began personally inspecting the smashana. At least, that is how it looked.

In the evening, when he and Devahuti were walking the dogs, he heard someone's heavy breathing, but apart from the dogs there were no other animals around. The sounds continued to accompany them on their evening walks for several more evenings, until at last he made out dark dense shadows abruptly changing direction, moving right through the trees. Focusing his inner sight, he discerned four semi-transparent, slightly blurring, enormous black beasts with short curly fur and eyes the color of wild honey. They were the size of two-month-old calves, with powerful paws, a wide chest, and saber-shaped tails. The muzzles resembled those of German shepherds, but were somewhat flattened. Two of the dogs went on sniffing the ground, the others stood motionless, staring straight at him; pale, ash-colored tongues moved rhythmically in time with the heavy breathing.

Turning to his sister, he whispered: "Do you see?" Devahuti nodded: "What are they?" "Only Datta Guru has four black dogs… don't look at them, let's go on…" They saw these semi-transparent beings several more times.

Soon after the installation of the Shivalingam, during the evening puja, he felt the presence of a certain force that produced a numbness both within and around him. The moment he noticed this, a sudden gust of wind shook the flowering bushes of bougainvillea, moving along the ground and spinning the bright petals into miniature whirlwinds. Then the gust died down; the sounds of the approaching night vanished, as if someone had switched them off. The sensations were strange, almost uncanny. Behind the columns of the temple a transparent figure appeared; passing toward the dhuni, the silhouette floated slowly back and stopped before the Shivalingam. Two of the three heads turned slightly toward the center of the temple. It was the immortal Guru Himself — Dattatreya.

About the wandering Deity it is known that daily, around four in the morning, He leaves Girnar and goes to Benares, where He bathes in the Ganga and performs japa and dhyana. [footnote] Dhyana — a state of utmost attention firmly fixed on the object of concentration, so that no other thought or object enters the mind. [/footnote] Then He goes to Kolhapur in Maharashtra…

results.english:
…and asks for alms in the guise of a wandering ascetic. From Kolhapur the Avatar of the Trinity goes to His birthplace, Mahurgad, where He eats the food collected in Kolhapur and rests before returning to Girnar. Did Dattatreya come to the smashana because the Shivalingam had been installed there? Could the lighting of the dhuni have been the signal that everything had been prepared for His arrival? What precisely had drawn the Avadhuta — the magic of the royal smashana, the presence of Shakti, or a combination of all the above factors?

However that may be, from that moment Dattatreya included Govardhan in His permanent route. Now He stopped each night at the temple of Shiva on the way to Girnar. Out of respect for the Deity, the evening puja was moved to a later hour, and at those hours no one approached the temple. Having learned that the Avatar of the Trimurti was visiting the smashana, two foreign disciples decided to await His arrival and, anticipating an adventure, settled behind the marble statue of Nandi [footnote] Nandi — the vahana, or bull, the mount of Lord Shiva. [/footnote] and began to wait.

The stone floor of the temple was still warm; the approaching night was slowly drawing on its dark garments, saturated with the fragrance of "night queen" flowers. The smell of burnt straw drifted in from the fields; in the temples the bells of puja began to ring. Some movement took place in the darkness, and both young men fell into a trance, as if someone had switched off their inner metronome. Coming to half an hour later, disoriented and bewildered, they could remember nothing. Hearing of their experiment, he said that to dance before a moving train is unwise; the human psyche is too fragile to bear the presence of Gods. Besides, eavesdropping was simply impolite.

Recalling the conversation with Ganganatha, he continued to analyze his words: "One of the sleepers, Rudra, has opened his eyes… The Avadhuta has come, to be seated at the cremation site of the Raja." At the time it had seemed to him that the Natha was referring to the arrival of Shiva, but Ganganatha was speaking of Dattatreya — only Guru Datta had three heads. Other events too acquired a new meaning: the lush growth of rare trees, the appearance of huge astral dogs, the sudden invasion of black datura flowers — all of this had been signs by Nature herself, announcing the manifestation of the Deity…

Linguistically the term "Avadhuta" comes from the roots dhu and ava — "to shake off," to cast off worldly bonds — and that is how a person becomes an Avadhuta, "the unattached," or literally "without bride." Such a title emphasizes certain aspects of the chosen path — the keeping of celibacy in the name of attaining liberation. The renunciation of worldly attachments is not exclusively a personal decision or the result of intellectual effort; it is a conditioned state of mind, the result of physical and spiritual practice. The decision to attain a state beyond mind comes of itself, spontaneously, naturally. In a wider sense the Avadhuta is a seeker who has appeared from nowhere — without roots and without causes, neither born nor unborn.

Dattatreya is the highest of the Avadhutas, attached to nothing, untouched by maya and yet fully immersed in life in all its totality. Being simultaneously Creator, Defender, and Destroyer, He embodies all the aspects of the Trimurti. Only one of His heads can be awake; the two other Deities slumber. "Switched off," they are "in standby mode" for the beginning of the next universal cycle, and the wakeful head takes upon itself a part of the duties of its sleeping companions. It is held that each particular yuga is ruled by one of the Gods of the Trinity: when Brahma is awake, Vishnu and Shiva sleep, and so on.

What had happened on the smashana suggested that a new era had already begun: Shiva appeared here first in person, then in the form of the Shivalingam, and this was followed by the arrival of the Avadhuta — Dattatreya. The Avatar of the Trinity confirmed Mahadeva's presence by the very fact of His evening visit to the smashana, by the testimony of His respect for the aspect of Shiva-Aghora… The onset of a new yuga had not been recorded in any calendar, but it had already begun, and meant a new era for the Earth and its inhabitants. He believed that this would be the yuga [footnote] Yuga — an age, an epoch. There are four yugas describing the evolution and involution of the Universe: Satya-yuga, Treta-yuga, Dvapara-yuga, and Kali-yuga. Shailendra Sharma associates with the yugas the four Indian varnas (castes). In his view, each varna was specific to a particular period of human history, and these tendencies extended far beyond the Indian subcontinent. The four varnas are the brahmins (priests), kshatriyas (warriors), vaishyas (merchants), and shudras (servants). [/footnote] of Yoga.

…Teaching him the sequences of the Shiva puja, the old pandit of Pashupatinath never once mentioned the ancient call of the Nathas. And yet now every ritual at the smashana began with the words "Alakh Niranjan!" These words burst out involuntarily when he first lifted the drum to begin the puja, and he did not resist the impulse.

The Sanskrit term "Alakshya" means "the one who cannot be seen," and "Niranjan" — "free of impurities" — and the Nathas use this term to describe the characteristics of the Creator, the Self, and the Atman, [footnote] Atman — the concept of the individual "I," which is not the soul and not the ego. [/footnote] invoking the formless, invisible, single essence sustaining all Creation. Rising above customary representations of terms and forms, every soul tries to return to the source of its origin, to find its roots, in order to continue the journey and reach the point of origin of the Universe itself. For him personally, "Alakh Niranjan" meant a call to the higher powers, the declaration that he, a yogin, was trying to go beyond the carbon-based Universe, to overcome physical and mental limitations and to attain freedom from all obstacles, including death itself.

For about a week the evening rituals began with the call "Alakh Niranjan" — but a few days later new words suddenly burst from him: "Alakh Datta, Guru Datta!" — the invocation of Dattatreya, who gave yoga to the world and remained invisible. Now the Guru revered by the Nathas was greeted before the rite of Shiva, and this greeting became part of the puja. He did not try to find any logical explanation for it; this logic was too deep and too embracing to be split into components. After the installation of the Shivalingam only a few years would pass — and Dattatreya would answer the call.

Guru Datta

Outside India Sri Dattatreya is almost unknown, although in Bharata millions worship Him. In the mass consciousness the Deity is perceived first of all as the incarnation of the three mightiest Gods, and only secondly as the Guru of Yoga. It was Dattatreya who stimulated the mutual influence and integration of the Vedas and Tantras, although such assertions do not always find support from contemporary religious scholars and academic researchers. To His authorship are attributed timeless texts and treatises, such as the Tripura Rahasya and the Avadhuta Gita, the "song of the free soul," which discloses the essence of the doctrine of non-duality.

The divine wandering ascetic and great yogin, Dattatreya never needed the help of a guru to attain enlightenment. He has no need either to free Himself from conditionings: He was deprived of them by virtue of His divine origin. Accompanied by dogs and a cow, Sri Dattatreya is the most mysterious deity of the Hindu pantheon. According to the pandits, the dogs accompanying Him personify the four Vedas, and the cow symbolizes Nature.

Strictly speaking, ancient India was never a spiritual paradise. Yes, Bharata was (and remains) the cradle of spirituality, but the number of spiritual seekers was not so great, and great saints and sages even fewer. The concepts of realization and liberation are known to many, but pursued only by some. The land is harsh, and the harshness of life demanded survival skills; enlightenment is sought only on a full stomach. The majority strove for worldly success and prosperity, and only at certain stages of life, after certain primary realizations, did people begin consciously to seek the guidance and support of enlightened teachers. In a word, the situation with spirituality was the same as it is today.

Guru Datta wore nothing but a loincloth, and His followers too saw no particular sense in clothing and possessions. For them these were unnecessary worldly conventions; "clad in the sky," the wandering ascetics merged effortlessly with the world around them. The highest ideal was a life in the bosom of Nature, outside society. Such an attitude helped them preserve the free, elemental nature of their own essence as well as an objective view of reality. Civilization gave birth to cities, which became centers of attraction for commercial activity, for commerce has always been the engine of social development — and the naked ascetics avoided cities long before they grew into megalopolises. They were convinced that, by acquiring the skills of urban survival, a person was doomed to lose his roots, to lose his natural power, character, freedom, and independence. They began their austerities by renouncing everything that hindered liberation: conventions, attachments, and dependences. But first one had to overcome the very fear of losing all that. The liberation of the soul was preceded by the liberation of consciousness from all limitations, and the body — the house of consciousness — also had to be stripped of all superfluous ornaments. When from the onion of the soul the clothing-husk fell away, the mind soared upward, overcoming all conventions.

Often the motives and way of life of the ancient yogins lay beyond the concepts comprehensible to laymen. The adepts succeeded in knowing and decoding the universal laws, reflecting them in the mirror of their own "I." Book-wisdom about yoga they held to be meaningless if the theory of liberation was not realized in practice.

In the teaching of Guru Datta there are only a few direct analogies with Christian ideals; there are no commandments and no didactic banalities woven into the stifling cocoon of dogmatic religious thought. Virtues were not propagated and not opposed to sins; instead, Dattatreya taught the essence of wisdom that frees a person from the sticky web of social obligations; social ostracism should not be feared, one simply had to step over it. To those who gradually learned to listen to the voice of the Earth and to live by Her laws, renunciation came naturally.

Once, on a nameless hill, Dattatreya gave the teaching [footnote] The teaching of Dattatreya to king Yadu is described in the Uddhava Gita, the 11th book of the Shrimad Bhagavatam. The text is a dialogue between Krishna and His greatest devotee Uddhava. [/footnote] to King Yadu, ancestor of the clan of Krishna. The name of the place was not mentioned, but it might easily have been the hill of Govardhan. Perhaps that is why Krishna worshipped the Hill as the place where His ancestors had been blessed with the teaching of Dattatreya.

Seeing the bliss-radiating 12-year-old boy, the king asked him about the secret of this blessed state and inquired about the name of his Guru. Dattatreya (for it was He) said that his Teacher was the Atman, but added that he had gained wisdom by observing 24 different beings, elements, and entities, of which only some were people. Nature herself was His Teacher, and he learned to do good from the earth, which humbly endures all wounds on its surface and yet gives forth harvests, trees, and fruits. Other teachers of Datta were the five elements and the planets, and innumerable lessons of wisdom were given to Him by the python and the honey-gatherer, the elephant and the deer, the fish and the courtesan, the raven and the child, the maiden and the snake, the blacksmith, the spider, the beetle, and the bee.

The jewel of Dattatreya's doctrine crystallized in the fire of His yogic tapas. The method of knowing reality He reduced to the denial of any concepts and ideas, since conflict and error were already inherent in any meanings and attempts to explain them. The simple, natural way of knowing of Natha-yoga is called "sahaj," learning without teaching. Thus, spontaneously and effortlessly, children come to know the…

teachings.teaching: An Avadhuta is one who has shaken off worldly bonds — not by intellectual decision but by physical and spiritual practice that makes renunciation arise spontaneously, naturally.
teachings.lens_note: The decision to go beyond mind comes of itself; you do not engineer it.

teachings.teaching: Each yuga is ruled by one of the Trimurti while the other two sleep — Dattatreya's three heads make this rotation visible; the awakened head shoulders the duties of the sleepers.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma reads the smashana evidence — Shiva, Shivalingam, then Dattatreya — as the signature of a new yuga of Yoga that has begun, uncalendered.

teachings.teaching: The 'Alakh Niranjan' call invokes the invisible, pure, formless single essence sustaining Creation — for the yogin it is the declaration of trying to step beyond the carbon-based Universe, beyond death itself.
teachings.lens_note: The Nathas' ancient cry became the spontaneous opening of the smashana puja; the body knew before the mind did.

teachings.teaching: Dattatreya's method is the denial of all concepts — wisdom is gained 'sahaj,' spontaneously, by observing the 24 teachers in Nature (earth, elements, animals, ordinary humans).
teachings.lens_note: Natha-yoga calls this learning-without-teaching; the earth that endures all wounds and still bears fruit is the model of doing good.

results.english:
…surrounding world. The yogin had to become a disciple of Nature and develop his awareness to a level on which he could learn the great wisdom from the tiniest insect or the mightiest ocean. Centuries later the disputes around the philosophical contradictions of the concepts of Dvaita and Advaita, [footnote] Dvaita and Advaita — denote duality and non-duality. Advaita maintains that the world is illusory. All actions and emotions are false impressions. In essence, the soul and God are one. When the soul is freed from this illusion, it merges with the Universal Consciousness. From the Dvaita point of view, the world is quite real. Although at first glance Dvaita and Advaita look very different, a more careful analysis shows that they represent only different points of view on one and the same reality. The difference lies in the level of self-realization of the questioner. [/footnote] duality and non-duality, continue. Dattatreya considered them both at once true and untrue. The Highest Truth cannot be classified or defined; none of the definitions can be applied to it. What is called Creation or Its manifestation cannot be a complete delusion of the mind, since It takes place in relative reality. At the same time, Creator and Creation already imply duality. For the yogin it is not really important whether to solve these unsolvable problems or simply to leave them entirely. When a person truly comes to know unity, duality and non-duality become for him only meaningless words.

…The newborn democracy greeted the fall of monarchies; it carried the ideas of individual freedom and the notion of human rights. And yet the power of capital remained sacred; the new construction of society had to be built around this fact. A newfangled social gadget, democracy still had to become better tuned, to establish true respect for personal choice and individual freedoms. Democracy was tailored so that its newfangled cut concealed the inborn deformities of the collective body of humanity. Capitalism was only a continuation of monarchy, a modernized version of the old impulse to suppress competitors and rule subjects — and corporations became the new empires, the symbols of a new reign. And yet democracy meant unquestionable progress, a victory over the old methods of tyranny and oppression. Together with changes in the four varnas of the ancient Vedic world order, the Vedic concept of the four yugas had also undergone change long before the start of the twentieth century. Absolutely everything was accelerating. The end of the Brahminical era of the rule of priests and scholar-brahmins was marked by the death of the legendary king Ravana, who reigned for 3.11 billion years. The era of the warrior kings-kshatriyas lasted 2 million years. The rule of the East India Company put an end to the era of kings: they were defeated by businessmen, vaishyas — which became the beginning of the time of the merchants.

By his reckoning, the new period would last 150–170 years, no longer. By 2050 the era of the Shudra-servants will be in full swing; any kind of service will become accessible to all and everyone. At present this sphere is developing so swiftly that all the signs of the next transition are evident.

But what did the Avatar of the Trimurti have to do with all this? Dattatreya embodied universal equality, symbolizing the balance of the elements, the equilibrium of the forces of creation, destruction, and preservation — as well as equality among all beings. The Avatar of the three Gods embodied the harmony of the forces stimulating the development of the human race, and harmonized the divine and human vibrations in unison with the heartbeat of the Earth.

The next round of the Divine play was called "Equality." The megalopolises had already begun to disgorge those who could not and would not continue to feed the mechanisms of the old system. The future was only a moment of eternity, and this was palpable. The change in Dattatreya's route meant that a new act of the Divine play had begun. The yogins had always said that the planet belongs equally to all living beings and to none in particular; the Earth itself set the algorithms of collective life-cycles. And, accordingly, every being had an equal right to existence.

…Whatever the future might be, he relied on the grace of Nature, learning daily from Her. Every creature around him was an expression of the consciousness of the enormous eternal Goddess. It remained to hope that Her favor, together with the grace of Dattatreya, would extend also to those who would receive the wisdom of this highest Teacher — the Earth.

The Grace of Devi

In the tradition of the Nathas, the particularities of the summoning of the Avadhuta are classified and for the most part masked under legend. The probability of an amateur attempt to call Him is small, and yet it is not excluded that someone might try to do so for practical considerations, devaluing the ritual and turning the sacred into another "convenience." Today the summoning of the Avadhuta is mere folklore; no one dares attempt it, and those who tried often did not survive to tell their stories.

All these years he had reflected on the sequence of events. His link with the Nathas had formed from the moment Ganganatha appeared at the smashana to see the Shivalingam and then asked to meet the man who had found it. All right, the visit of the Nathas could have been chance. But the invitation to meet the head of the order Matsyendranatha was by no means a matter of chance. Perhaps all these events framed a much vaster picture? Perhaps he was standing too close to make out the whole composition. It had taken him more than twenty years to uncover the hidden meanings, and the truth was at once majestic and sobering.

The discovery of the natural Shivalingam was a direct grace of Shiva. It was He who ordered the search to begin; it was His voice and His will. The hill of Govardhan, the old smashana, and the yogic sadhana were important pieces of the puzzle, and yet the central figure of the story was Adi Shakti. The Universal Mother was the magnet, the source, the incantation. The Goddess spoke through signs and symbols, spirits and gods. Appearing first in the form of a small statuette, She had left Her innermost, unmistakably feminine symbols in the form of stone fragments to show the way to Her eternal companion. And Shiva followed Her.

But why, in all of vast India, had Adi Shakti chosen this very place? The answer to this question lay in Her recognition of the tradition of Babaji. By coming, She was blessing this yogic line, expressing Her approval. Because precisely this tradition initiated women into the art of spiritual mastery, granting them, without any discrimination, a chance to discover their Higher Self. And precisely for this reason the parampara had earned the trust of the Supreme Goddess.

Babaji's initiation of the householder Lahiri Mahasaya into the almost occult art of Kriya yoga opened the doors for the spiritual development of women. In the 19th century they could not receive initiation into any tradition without the consent and support of family and relatives. The independent search for a spiritual teacher was simply unsafe. Too many dangers awaited women at every step: the harassment of false saints, insults in unfamiliar places, etc. Apart from a few unique cases, Indian women remained in spiritual isolation, given over to the mercy of pandits, confined within rigid frameworks of religious rituals.

Although the Bhagavad Gita spoke of the accessibility of spiritual teaching to all, the real state of affairs was far from ideal. Babaji took care that the wives, mothers, daughters, and sisters of yogins could study Kriya yoga together with their husbands, fathers, brothers, and sons. The parampara became a refuge for female students; it protected its spiritual daughters. Lahiri Mahasaya was a fierce champion of universal education: together with his comrades he opened a school for girls, despite considerable resistance from the conservative-minded public. At the end of the 19th century, in India, even middle-class families would not allow girls to attend lessons in state schools because of various dangers. Rapes and abductions were so common that even letting daughters out of the house without escort was a risk. Lahiri Mahasaya and his companions managed to open the school, and it continues to function today as a preparatory college.

The more he reflected, the more sense this hypothesis made. Babaji was the first Guru openly to show respect for women. In the line of Mahavatar, men and women were given equal opportunities and the same techniques. And this was a rarity in the predominantly masculine world of yoga.

Adi Shakti is the invisible force, the life-giving Prana, the one who gives Life; She walks eternally beside Time and Death. One of Mahadeva's many dwellings, the smashana, was waiting for its Mistress, and She began the housecleaning, clearing space for the coming of the Master. The discovered stone fragments, the signs of the Goddess's presence, were by no means an accidental find. Although ancient eternal forces care little for self-promotion, the concentration of such energies still leaves certain marks, just as the foot leaves a trace in the sand.

…Shiva followed Her, and this caused a great resonance both in the astral world and among mortal and immortal Siddhas. The three Nathas had come here to see this with their own eyes. The words of praise spoken by Ganganatha were addressed not to the man who had found a sacred relic by accident, but to the Fifth Guru of the tradition of Babaji, chosen for the installation here of the symbol of Shiva.

The Nathas followed a centuries-old tradition in which women were regarded primarily as an obstacle on the path to enlightenment. With time, yoga and femininity became antipodes. The orthodox doctrines of the Nathas turned the very subject of feminine force into taboo. Even Matsyendranatha had earned a bad reputation among His followers for honoring women instead of condemning any connection with them. And yet, as followers of Shiva, the Nathas could not ignore the role of Shakti, for without Her there could be no Shiva either.

In many yogic lines the abstract grace of the Great Goddess was valued, was sought after; She is the companion of Mahadeva. But in real life everything feminine had to be trimmed, uprooted, expelled. For beginning Natha-novices worldly temptations were too great, and it was simpler to declare women evil so as not to stray from the path of virtue.

It seemed that, having confirmed all the facts gathered by His scouts, Guru Matsyendranatha had decided to summon him to Mahurgad for entirely practical considerations. The special instructions given by the immortal Siddha helped him both to continue yoga-sadhana successfully and to worship the Avadhuta. When everything had been arranged for His arrival, Dattatreya — the Kula [footnote] Kula — a community of people who follow similar cultural rituals, often worshipping the same god. Kula corresponds to the notion of "family" or to modern notions of "clan." [/footnote] guru of the order of Nathas — came onto the scene.

…It is curious that all these great forces and beings came of their own free will. He had never tried to summon any of them, well understanding the danger of such attempts. Yes, he worshipped the Mahavatar and admired Him, but never tried to call Him. The Master himself knocked at the door of his room, himself gave instructions for new levels of kriya, without him even asking for it. Everything was decided and predetermined; he simply had to play along, continuing to fulfill the duties laid upon him.

teachings.teaching: Dvaita and Advaita are both at once true and untrue; the Highest Truth cannot be classified — for the yogin who truly knows unity, duality and non-duality are only meaningless words.
teachings.lens_note: Dattatreya's lens: the dispute itself dissolves at the level of self-realization; argument is the work of an unrealized questioner.

teachings.teaching: History is a procession of varnas: Brahmin priests, then warrior kshatriyas (ended by the East India Company), then merchant vaishyas (the current ~150–170 year period), then shudra-servants (full swing by 2050).
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's mapping of yuga to varna applies globally, not just to India; the next 'Equality' round is the Earth's algorithm asserting itself.

teachings.teaching: The Goddess chose Govardhan because Babaji's parampara is the one tradition that initiated women equally — without that openness, She would not have come.
teachings.lens_note: The full causal chain: women's spiritual rights → Adi Shakti's approval → Her arrival → Shiva's pursuit → Shivalingam's discovery → Dattatreya's awakening.

teachings.teaching: Great forces never come because you summon them; they come of their own free will. The yogi's job is to be ready when they knock, and to play along.
teachings.lens_note: Babaji knocked uninvited; this is the inversion of the tantric model of forceful summoning. Sharma's whole biography is the receiving end.
